exemplar.title:
How does the environmental quality improve moving along a transect from the CBD to the
urban-rural fringe of Stuttgart?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How does the environmental quality improve moving along a transect from the CBD to the
urban-rural fringe of Stuttgart?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with direct theoretical links allowing hypothesis formation
Geographic context is precise, with Stuttgart transect and feasibility of primary data collection well established
Multiple locator maps follow conventions and enable clear spatial orientation
weaknesses: Terminology of âenvironmental qualityâ could be refined by specifying measurable indicators
Minor cartographic conventions are missing (north arrow/scale bar on one locator map)
Cross-section map could include elevation annotations for fuller context
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Criterion A reach the highest band. The research question is fully geographical, supported by three testable hypotheses. The geographic context is detailed and siteâspecific, and mapping conventions are largely observed. Minor omissions in mapping detail do not undermine the overall excellence in question formulation and context.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods (stratified sampling, three daily time slots) are clearly described and linked to the research hypotheses
Instruments and field survey were correctly implemented across 12 sites, yielding usable primary data
weaknesses: Justification for stratification boundaries and theoretical basis of chosen indicators is lacking
Outlier treatment is asserted but not methodologically explained
Data collection is limited to a singleâday snapshot, affecting reliability and coverage
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The description of methods and their relevance meets the criteria for Band 2âprocedures are clear and data are adequate but lack depth in theoretical alignment and procedural justification. Data collection techniques are properly used but yield only moderately reliable data.

strengths: Data collected (COâ, noise, temperature, survey, traffic counts) are directly relevant to the research question
Presentation techniques (tables, line graphs, radar plots) are appropriate and support interpretation
Conventions (units, labelling, keys) are generally adhered to, with only occasional minor lapses
weaknesses: Some figures lack variability measures (standard deviations, error bars) needed for full assessment of data reliability
Bibliographic conventions are inconsistent and should be standardized
Inclusion of tangential data (GDP, vehicleâper-capita) diverts focus from transect analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Most data are directly relevant and presented with appropriate and mostly effective techniques. Occasional errors in conventions and missing measures of dispersion prevent Band 6 performance but the work comfortably exceeds basic sufficiency, meriting 5 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Use of averages, percentage change, and Spearmanâs rank correlation demonstrates appropriate statistical techniques
Interpretation identifies key trends and anomalies (e.g., open space ratings) and links these to hypotheses and urbanâgeographical theory
Analysis addresses each hypothesis with coherent reasoning and some triangulation with secondary data
weaknesses: Statistical significance is asserted without formal testing (e.g., confidence intervals or p-values)
Linkages to wider urban models are present but not fully developed for all variables
Analysis has minor gaps (e.g., no seasonal assessment, incomplete noiseâtraffic correlation)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis employs appropriate descriptive and statistical techniques and links trends to theory. While not fully comprehensive (missing formal confidence calculations and some theoretical depth), it surpasses basic descriptive treatment, justifying a midâhigh Band 5-6 score (6 marks).

strengths: Conclusion clearly restates observed patterns and directly addresses the hypotheses
Supports conclusions with data trends identified in the analysis
weaknesses: Support is partialâCOâ and noise results remain inconclusive without alternative explanations
Causation is sometimes implied from correlation without acknowledging limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear and references analytical findings, meeting the criteria for a fully supported conclusion. However, limited exploration of alternative explanations and partial support for some variables constrains the mark to 2.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant strengths and weaknesses that impact data quality and reliability
Improvement suggestions (seasonal sampling, increased sample size, methodological refinements) are directly linked to reliability and breadth
Discussion demonstrates awareness of instrument and design limitations
weaknesses: Feasibility details for proposed improvements (e.g., scheduling, resource implications) are limited
Some suggestions could be more specific (e.g., statistical criteria for outlier removal)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation comprehensively explains methodological strengths and weaknesses and provides detailed improvements with clear impact on data reliability. Although feasibility could be expanded, the criterionâs highest band is met, justifying 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple locator maps are provided (national, city, site, cross-section). Each map contains orientation, a form of scale, clear labelling and a key, meeting standard cartographic conventions and allowing the reader to visualise the sampling layout unambiguously.

The research question is clearly geographical, focused on a spatial change in environmental quality along an urban transect, and is explicitly related to the Urban Environments syllabus section. Three testable hypotheses are derived, demonstrating a clear theoretical basis for investigation.

Tables, line graphs, radar plots and photographs are appropriate and allow interpretation, yet some figures lack full axis labels or optimal choice of graph type; presentation is solid rather than the âmost effectiveâ possible.

Primary techniques were correctly implemented at 12 sites and repeated three times; instruments and surveys generated usable data. However, coverage is still modest (single-day snapshot, only two sites per pair) and the write-up concedes possible reliability issues, so data are adequate but not of consistently high quality.

The student makes use of averages, percentage change and Spearmanâs rank correlations, moving beyond mere description. Confidence levels are mentioned but not formally calculated; therefore the statistical treatment is appropriate yet not fully comprehensive.

Trends, anomalies and limited correlations are identified and related to hypotheses and some urban-geographical ideas (bowl topography, proximity to roads). However, theoretical discussion is brief and linkage between all variables and wider urban models is not exhaustive.

The student specifies Stuttgart and pinpoints a transect that begins in the CBD and runs to the urban-rural fringe, supported by discussion of Stuttgartâs âbowlâ topography and traffic corridors. The scale and accessibility of 12 primary data sites confirm feasibility for fieldwork.

Strengths (multiple indicators, repeated readings) and weaknesses (single day, instrument consistency) are outlined with relevance; explanations of impacts are present but not fully developed across all methodological stages.

The data set (COâ, noise, temperature, EQ survey, traffic counts) is largely relevant to the research question and generally sufficient to attempt analysis; minor gaps (short transect, seasonal limitation) remain but most strands of the enquiry can be explored.

A single concluding section restates patterns and compares them with hypotheses, but support is partial: COâ and noise results are labelled inconclusive, and reasoning relies on speculation about site proximity rather than strong analytical evidence.

Each hypothesis is addressed, but gaps persist: limited explanation for lack of COâ gradient, no seasonal assessment, and modest triangulation with secondary data. The analysis is generally coherent yet not free from omissions.

Methods (stratified sampling, three daily time slots, COâ/sound/temperature readings, field ranking survey) are described and linked to the hypotheses, but explanations lack depth on theoretical alignment (e.g. why those indicators best reflect âenvironmental qualityâ) and some procedural detail is brief.

Units are usually shown and maps include north arrows/keys, but small inconsistencies (site numbers omitted in captions, minor typographic errors in tables) occur. Overall conventions are respected with only occasional lapses.

Several improvement ideas are proposed (seasonal sampling, larger data set, extra hypotheses) and briefly linked to how they would enhance reliability or breadth. The discussion is appropriate but lacks detailed consideration of feasibility and specific methodological adjustments.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical, focusing on spatial change in environmental quality along an urban transect and linking explicitly to the Urban Environments syllabus, supporting hypothesis formation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Consider refining the term âenvironmental qualityâ in the question by specifying indicators (e.g. air pollutant concentration, noise level, green space coverage) for greater precision and measurability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The student provides rich geographic context, mentioning Stuttgartâs automotive hub status and topography, which grounds the fieldwork in a realistic urban setting.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The locator map (Figure 1) lacks a north arrow and scale bar. Adding these elements would enhance cartographic conventions and spatial orientation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The inclusion of GDP and vehicle-per-capita statistics, while interesting, diverts focus from environmental quality. Streamline to retain only data directly relevant to the transect study.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The discussion of the âKesselâ topography effectively links urban form with pollution trapping, demonstrating theoretical understanding.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 2âs map follows conventions but would benefit from a scale bar and clearer grid reference to support primary data site identification.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The cross-section map (Figure 3) clearly shows site positions along the transect but could be improved by annotating elevation values to contextualize environmental gradients.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Restatement of the research question here duplicates earlier sections. Consider moving directly into hypothesis linkage to improve flow.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The open space rating trend on page 14 effectively confirms Hypothesis 3, linking data to spatial urban pattern and theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: On page 17 the paragraph ends abruptly, suggesting missing text; ensure the analysis is complete and cohesive.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The conclusion attributes causation to correlations without acknowledging alternative explanations or limitations in design.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: Evaluation outlines seasonal sampling and tool consistency improvements, demonstrating awareness of methodological limitations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Further detail on feasibility and specific scheduling for additional trials would strengthen the evaluationâs improvement suggestions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: The bibliography contains inconsistent citation styles and missing publisher details; standardise using a recognized style (e.g. APA or Harvard).
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 is well articulated, connecting CO$_2$ emission reductions to decreased traffic intensity and industrial activity away from the CBD.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 2 would be stronger if it specified how many decibels of reduction are expected per site interval or linked to urban sound models.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The methodology paragraph describes stratified sampling but omits justification for site selection stratification criteria and subgroup boundaries.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: A strength of the fieldwork is repeating environmental readings at three daily time slots and averaging the results, which enhances the reliability of primary data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The justification claims âno outliersâ without describing how outliers were identified or removed. Include a statistical criterion for outlier treatment.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Table 2 presents average CO$_2$ but omits measures of dispersion (e.g. standard deviation), which are necessary to assess data variability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure 5 line graph lacks error bars or confidence intervals; adding these would support interpretation of CO$_2$ trends.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The interpretation of CO$_2$ data cites an acceptable range but does not statistically test whether observed site differences are significant.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The noise data table (Table 3) provides comprehensive decibel readings but again lacks variability measures or statistical test results.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Noise analysis notes proximity to roads but omits testing correlation between noise and traffic counts; include Spearmanâs or Pearsonâs test for rigor.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis
